1:
"And I saw a new heaven and a new earth: for the first heaven and the first earth were passed away; and there was no more sea." The witnesses note the new heaven and new earth — the crux of renewal or new creation. On "I saw a new heaven and a new earth: for the first heaven and the first earth were passed away," interpreters note the renewal or replacement of the cosmos. The grammatical-historical Barnes (with the latin-fathers Victorinus and the patristic tradition) holds that the "first heaven and first earth passed away" not by annihilation but by renovation — the present world is purified and changed for the better (by the conflagration, 2 Peter 3:10-13); Victorinus reads the world passing away "by the conflagration of the supernal fire," the earth changed for the better, not annihilated. A second reading takes the passing away as the destruction of the old creation and the making of a wholly new one; the Reformed Gill notes the new heaven and earth as the final, glorious state succeeding the old.

On "a new heaven and a new earth," the witnesses note the renewed creation. Barnes and Ellicott note that "a new heaven and a new earth" (Isaiah 65:17; 66:22; 2 Peter 3:13) is the renewed, glorified creation, the final abode of the righteous; the new order succeeding the old, fitted for the redeemed.

On "there was no more sea," the grammatical-historical note the absence of the sea: "there was no more sea" — the sea (the symbol of instability, separation, and the restless nations) is gone; whether literal or symbolic, its absence marks the perfect peace and unity of the new creation; no more division or unrest. The verse displays the new heaven and new earth.

The witnesses agree the substance: the first heaven and earth pass away, and a new heaven and new earth appear, with no more sea — the final, glorious order (Barnes, Gill, Victorinus). The mode bears a crux: the renovation/purification of the present cosmos by fire, changed for the better, not annihilated (Victorinus, Barnes), or the destruction of the old and a wholly new creation. The verse displays the new-creation crux. The verse marks the heaven: new. The verse displays the earth: new. The verse displays the debate: renewal or new creation. The verse displays the sea: no more. The vision displays the renewed creation succeeding the old. The verse displays the first: passed away. The verse displays the new: appearing. The verse displays the sea: gone. The new heaven and new earth display the consummation of all things: the first heaven and earth pass away, and a new heaven and new earth appear, with no more sea — read by some as the renovation and purification of the present cosmos (Victorinus, Barnes), by others as a wholly new creation; the final, glorious abode of the righteous. The verse displays the new creation, with the New Jerusalem descending (v.2).
2:
"And I John saw the holy city, new Jerusalem, coming down from God out of heaven, prepared as a bride adorned for her husband." The witnesses note the New Jerusalem descending as a bride. On "I John saw the holy city, new Jerusalem, coming down from God out of heaven," the grammatical voices note the descending city. Barnes and Robertson note the vision: "the holy city, new Jerusalem, coming down from God out of heaven" — John sees the holy city, the new Jerusalem, descending from God out of heaven; the city is the dwelling of God with the redeemed (v.3), the glorified church/community of the saved; it comes down from God, of heavenly origin and gift.

On "the holy city, new Jerusalem," the witnesses note the figure. Barnes and Ellicott note that "the holy city, new Jerusalem" (3:12; Galatians 4:26; Hebrews 12:22) is the figure of the redeemed people of God, the glorified church, in their final, blessed state; the new Jerusalem, in contrast to fallen Babylon (ch. 17-18); the holy city of God's dwelling.

On "prepared as a bride adorned for her husband," the grammatical-historical note the bride-figure: the city is "prepared as a bride adorned for her husband" — the new Jerusalem is the bride of the Lamb (v.9; 19:7), adorned and ready for her husband (Christ); the church as the holy city and the bride are one; the figure of the redeemed prepared in beauty for union with Christ. The verse displays the New Jerusalem descending as a bride.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the city — "the holy city, new Jerusalem, coming down from God out of heaven," the glorified church/city descending from God (Barnes, Robertson); "the holy city, new Jerusalem" the redeemed people of God, over against Babylon (Galatians 4:26; Hebrews 12:22, Barnes, Ellicott); "prepared as a bride adorned for her husband," the city the bride of the Lamb, adorned for Christ (19:7, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the holy city. The verse marks the city: new Jerusalem. The verse displays the descent: from God. The verse displays the figure: a bride. The verse displays the adornment: for her husband. The vision displays the glorified church descending as Christ's bride. The verse displays the city: holy. The verse displays the bride: adorned. The verse displays the husband: Christ. The New Jerusalem descending as a bride displays the glorious counterpart to Babylon: John sees the holy city, new Jerusalem, coming down from God out of heaven, prepared as a bride adorned for her husband — the glorified church, the dwelling of God, presented as the Lamb's bride. The verse displays the descending city, where God dwells with men (v.3).
3:
"And I heard a great voice out of heaven saying, Behold, the tabernacle of God is with men, and he will dwell with them, and they shall be his people, and God himself shall be with them, and be their God." The witnesses note God dwelling with his people in the new creation. On "Behold, the tabernacle of God is with men, and he will dwell with them," the grammatical voices note the dwelling of God. Barnes and Robertson note the proclamation: "Behold, the tabernacle of God is with men, and he will dwell (tabernacle) with them" — a great voice announces that God's tabernacle (dwelling) is now with men; God himself dwells among the redeemed; the consummation of the covenant promise — God with his people (the fulfillment of the tabernacle and temple, John 1:14; Ezekiel 37:27).

On "they shall be his people, and God himself shall be with them, and be their God," the witnesses note the covenant fulfilled. Barnes and Ellicott note the covenant formula: "they shall be his people, and God himself shall be with them, and be their God" — the ancient covenant promise (Leviticus 26:11-12; Jeremiah 31:33) is consummated; God dwells with his people, they are his, and he is their God; the perfect, eternal communion of God and the redeemed.

On the dwelling, the grammatical-historical note the blessedness: the essence of the new creation's joy is God's immediate presence — God himself with his people; the tabernacle of God with men is the heart of the blessed state; the redeemed enjoy unbroken fellowship with God. The verse displays God dwelling with his people in the new creation.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the dwelling — "Behold, the tabernacle of God is with men, and he will dwell with them," God dwelling among the redeemed, the covenant consummated (John 1:14; Ezekiel 37:27, Barnes, Robertson); "they shall be his people, and God himself shall be with them, and be their God," the covenant formula fulfilled (Leviticus 26:11-12; Jeremiah 31:33, Barnes, Ellicott); the immediate presence of God the heart of the blessed state (grammatical-historical). The verse displays God with his people. The verse marks the tabernacle: with men. The verse displays the dwelling: God with them. The verse displays the covenant: his people, their God. The verse displays the blessedness: God's presence. The proclamation displays God dwelling in perfect communion with the redeemed. The verse displays the tabernacle: of God. The verse displays the people: his. The verse displays the God: with them. God dwelling with his people in the new creation displays the heart of the blessed state: the tabernacle of God is with men, he dwells with them, they are his people, and God himself is with them and is their God — the consummated covenant, the perfect communion of God and the redeemed. The verse displays the indwelling God, who wipes away every tear (v.4).
4:
"And God shall wipe away all tears from their eyes; and there shall be no more death, neither sorrow, nor crying, neither shall there be any more pain: for the former things are passed away." The witnesses note the end of death, sorrow, and pain in the new creation. On "God shall wipe away all tears from their eyes," the grammatical voices note the comfort. Barnes and Robertson note the tender promise: "God shall wipe away all tears from their eyes" — God himself wipes away every tear from the eyes of the redeemed (7:17; Isaiah 25:8); the figure of perfect comfort and the end of all sorrow; God's own hand removes their grief.

On "there shall be no more death, neither sorrow, nor crying, neither shall there be any more pain," the witnesses note the abolition of evils. Barnes and Ellicott note the catalogue of what is gone: "no more death, neither sorrow, nor crying, neither... any more pain" — death, sorrow, crying, and pain — all the evils and miseries of the old order — are abolished in the new creation; the redeemed are forever free from suffering and death.

On "for the former things are passed away," the grammatical-historical note the passing of the old: "for the former things are passed away" — the old order, with all its evils, has passed; the former things (the world of sin, death, and sorrow) are gone; the new creation is wholly free of them; the basis of the abolition of suffering is the passing of the old world. The verse displays the end of death, sorrow, and pain in the new creation.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the comfort — "God shall wipe away all tears from their eyes," God removing all sorrow (7:17; Isaiah 25:8, Barnes, Robertson); "no more death, neither sorrow, nor crying, neither... pain," the abolition of all the evils of the old order (Barnes, Ellicott); "for the former things are passed away," the old world with its evils gone (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the end of sorrow. The verse marks the tears: wiped away. The verse displays the death: no more. The verse displays the sorrow: gone. The verse displays the former things: passed. The promise displays the redeemed forever free of suffering and death. The verse displays the tears: removed. The verse displays the pain: ended. The verse displays the old: passed. The end of death, sorrow, and pain in the new creation displays the blessedness of the redeemed: God wipes away every tear, and there is no more death, sorrow, crying, or pain, for the former things are passed away — the perfect, eternal freedom from all suffering. The verse displays the comforted redeemed, as God makes all things new (v.5).
5:
"And he that sat upon the throne said, Behold, I make all things new. And he said unto me, Write: for these words are true and faithful." The witnesses note God's declaration that he makes all things new. On "he that sat upon the throne said, Behold, I make all things new," the grammatical voices note the divine declaration. Barnes and Robertson note the proclamation from the throne: "he that sat upon the throne said, Behold, I make all things new" — God himself, from the throne, declares that he makes all things new; the renewal of all creation (cf. 2 Corinthians 5:17; Isaiah 43:19); the comprehensive renovation/recreation of all things in the new order; God is the author of the new creation.

On "Behold, I make all things new," the witnesses note the scope. Barnes and Ellicott note that "I make all things new" — the whole order is renewed; not merely new things, but all things made new; the transformation of the entire creation into the glorified state; God's renewing work embraces all.

On "Write: for these words are true and faithful," the grammatical-historical note the assurance: "Write: for these words are true and faithful" — God bids John write, affirming that these words are true and faithful (trustworthy, genuine); the promises of the new creation are sure and certain; God's word guarantees their fulfillment; the reader is assured of the truth of these things. The verse displays God's declaration that he makes all things new.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the declaration — "he that sat upon the throne said, Behold, I make all things new," God declaring the renewal of all things (2 Corinthians 5:17; Isaiah 43:19, Barnes, Robertson); "I make all things new" the comprehensive renovation of all (Barnes, Ellicott); "Write: for these words are true and faithful," the assurance of the sure, certain promises (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the renewing God. The verse marks the throne: God speaking. The verse displays the declaration: all things new. The verse displays the scope: all things. The verse displays the assurance: true and faithful. The proclamation displays God making all creation new. The verse displays the new: made. The verse displays the words: true. The verse displays the writing: commanded. God's declaration that he makes all things new displays the divine work of the consummation: he who sits on the throne says, "Behold, I make all things new," and bids John write, for these words are true and faithful — God renewing all creation, his promise sure. The verse displays the renewing God, the Alpha and Omega (v.6).
6:
"And he said unto me, It is done. I am Alpha and Omega, the beginning and the end. I will give unto him that is athirst of the fountain of the water of life freely." The witnesses note God as Alpha and Omega and the water of life. On "It is done. I am Alpha and Omega, the beginning and the end," the grammatical voices note the divine declaration. Barnes and Robertson note the proclamation: "It is done. I am Alpha and Omega, the beginning and the end" — God declares "It is done" (the work of the new creation completed, as 16:17), and proclaims himself "Alpha and Omega, the beginning and the end" (1:8; 22:13) — the first and last, the eternal God who begins and consummates all things.

On "I am Alpha and Omega, the beginning and the end," the witnesses note God's eternity. Barnes and Ellicott note that "Alpha and Omega, the beginning and the end" — the first and last letters of the Greek alphabet — affirm God's eternity and his sovereignty over all history; he is the origin and goal of all; the One who completes what he began.

On "I will give unto him that is athirst of the fountain of the water of life freely," the Reformed Gill and grammatical-historical note the gracious offer: "I will give unto him that is athirst of the fountain of the water of life freely" — to the thirsty, God gives freely of the fountain of the water of life (22:17; John 4:14; Isaiah 55:1); the free gift of eternal life and the satisfaction of the soul's thirst; the gracious invitation amid the consummation. The verse displays God as Alpha and Omega and the water of life.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the declaration — "It is done. I am Alpha and Omega, the beginning and the end," the completed work and the eternal God (16:17; 1:8; 22:13, Barnes, Robertson); "Alpha and Omega, the beginning and the end," God's eternity and sovereignty over all (Barnes, Ellicott); "I will give unto him that is athirst of the fountain of the water of life freely," the free gift of eternal life to the thirsty (22:17; John 4:14; Isaiah 55:1, Gill). The verse displays the eternal God and his gift. The verse marks the work: done. The verse displays the title: Alpha and Omega. The verse displays the gift: the water of life. The verse displays the offer: freely. The declaration displays the eternal God freely giving the water of life. The verse displays the done: completed. The verse displays the Alpha: and Omega. The verse displays the water: of life. God as Alpha and Omega and the water of life displays the divine declaration at the consummation: "It is done. I am Alpha and Omega, the beginning and the end" — and God freely gives the thirsty of the fountain of the water of life; the eternal God who completes all and gives eternal life freely. The verse displays the giving God, with the inheritance of the overcomer (v.7).
7:
"He that overcometh shall inherit all things; and I will be his God, and he shall be my son." The very brief verse notes the overcomer's inheritance and sonship. The grammatical voices note the promise. Barnes and Robertson note the reward: "He that overcometh shall inherit all things; and I will be his God, and he shall be my son" — the one who overcomes (the victorious believer, as in the seven letters) shall inherit all things, and God will be his God and he God's son; the consummate reward — the inheritance of all the blessings of the new creation, and the relation of sonship to God.

On "shall inherit all things," the witnesses note the inheritance. Barnes and Ellicott note that the overcomer "shall inherit all things" — he is heir of all the blessings of the new creation, the holy city, and the presence of God; the comprehensive inheritance of the redeemed (cf. Romans 8:17; Matthew 5:5).

On "I will be his God, and he shall be my son," the grammatical-historical note the covenant sonship: "I will be his God, and he shall be my son" — the covenant relation (2 Samuel 7:14; cf. the covenant formula, v.3) is applied to the overcomer; God is his God, and he is God's son; the intimate, filial relation of the redeemed to God; the adoption consummated. The verse displays the overcomer's inheritance and sonship.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the reward — "He that overcometh shall inherit all things; and I will be his God, and he shall be my son," the overcomer's inheritance and sonship (Barnes, Robertson); "shall inherit all things," the comprehensive inheritance of the redeemed (Romans 8:17, Barnes, Ellicott); "I will be his God, and he shall be my son," the covenant sonship consummated (2 Samuel 7:14, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the overcomer's reward. The verse marks the overcomer: inheriting. The verse displays the inheritance: all things. The verse displays the relation: God and son. The verse displays the adoption: consummated. The promise displays the overcomer heir of all and son of God. The verse displays the overcomer: rewarded. The verse displays the inheritance: full. The verse displays the sonship: granted. The overcomer's inheritance and sonship display the promise to the victorious: he that overcomes shall inherit all things, and God will be his God and he God's son — the consummate inheritance of the new creation and the filial relation to God. The verse displays the overcomer's reward, set against the doom of the lost (v.8).
8:
"But the fearful, and unbelieving, and the abominable, and murderers, and whoremongers, and sorcerers, and idolaters, and all liars, shall have their part in the lake which burneth with fire and brimstone: which is the second death." The witnesses note the catalogue of the lost in the second death. On "the fearful, and unbelieving, and the abominable, and murderers," the grammatical voices note the excluded. Barnes and Robertson note the contrast with the overcomer (v.7): "the fearful, and unbelieving, and the abominable, and murderers, and whoremongers, and sorcerers, and idolaters, and all liars" — over against the overcomer who inherits all, this catalogue of sinners (the cowardly/fearful, unbelieving, abominable, murderers, fornicators, sorcerers, idolaters, liars) are excluded; the list of the unredeemed who have no part in the new creation.

On "the fearful, and unbelieving," the witnesses note the first-named. Barnes and Ellicott note that "the fearful (cowardly), and unbelieving" head the list — those who, through cowardice or unbelief, fall away or refuse Christ; their fear of man and lack of faith condemn them; the catalogue then names the grosser sins.

On "shall have their part in the lake which burneth with fire and brimstone: which is the second death," the grammatical-historical note the doom: such "shall have their part in the lake which burneth with fire and brimstone: which is the second death" — the lost are assigned to the lake of fire, the second death (20:14); their portion is eternal perdition; the solemn contrast to the overcomer's inheritance; exclusion from the new creation and consignment to the second death. The verse displays the catalogue of the lost in the second death.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the excluded — "the fearful, and unbelieving, and the abominable, and murderers, and whoremongers, and sorcerers, and idolaters, and all liars," the catalogue of sinners excluded from the new creation (Barnes, Robertson); "the fearful, and unbelieving," the cowardly and faithless heading the list (Barnes, Ellicott); "their part in the lake which burneth with fire and brimstone: which is the second death," the doom of the lost (20:14, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the lost. The verse marks the catalogue: the sinners. The verse displays the first: fearful, unbelieving. The verse displays the doom: the lake of fire. The verse displays the finality: the second death. The catalogue displays the unredeemed consigned to the second death. The verse displays the sinners: excluded. The verse displays the lake: of fire. The verse displays the death: second. The catalogue of the lost in the second death displays the solemn contrast to the overcomer: the fearful, unbelieving, abominable, murderers, fornicators, sorcerers, idolaters, and all liars have their part in the lake of fire and brimstone, the second death — excluded from the new creation, consigned to eternal perdition. The verse displays the lost, before the vision of the bride (v.9).
9:
"And there came unto me one of the seven angels which had the seven vials full of the seven last plagues, and talked with me, saying, Come hither, I will shew thee the bride, the Lamb's wife." The witnesses note the angel offering to show the bride, the Lamb's wife. On "one of the seven angels which had the seven vials... saying, Come hither, I will shew thee the bride, the Lamb's wife," the grammatical voices note the invitation. Barnes and Robertson note the parallel to ch. 17: "one of the seven angels which had the seven vials full of the seven last plagues... Come hither, I will shew thee the bride, the Lamb's wife" — one of the vial-angels (as the one who showed the harlot, 17:1) now invites John to see "the bride, the Lamb's wife"; the deliberate contrast — the same kind of angel shows the harlot Babylon and the bride the New Jerusalem.

On "the bride, the Lamb's wife," the witnesses note the figure. Barnes and Henry note that "the bride, the Lamb's wife" is the New Jerusalem (v.2, 10), the glorified church; the bride of the Lamb (19:7); the holy city and the bride are one — the redeemed people of God in their final, glorious union with Christ.

On the contrast, the grammatical-historical note the parallel: the angel who showed the judgment of the great whore (17:1) now shows the bride; the New Jerusalem (the faithful bride) is set over against Babylon (the unfaithful harlot); the deliberate contrast crowns the book — the doom of the harlot and the glory of the bride. The verse displays the angel offering to show the bride, the Lamb's wife.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the invitation — "one of the seven angels which had the seven vials... Come hither, I will shew thee the bride, the Lamb's wife," the vial-angel inviting John to see the bride, paralleling the showing of the harlot (17:1, Barnes, Robertson); "the bride, the Lamb's wife" the New Jerusalem, the glorified church (v.2; 19:7, Barnes, Henry); the deliberate contrast of the bride and the harlot Babylon (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the bride-vision. The verse marks the angel: of the vials. The verse displays the offer: the bride. The verse displays the figure: the Lamb's wife. The verse displays the contrast: with the harlot. The invitation displays the bride shown over against Babylon. The verse displays the angel: inviting. The verse displays the bride: shown. The verse displays the wife: the Lamb's. The angel offering to show the bride, the Lamb's wife, displays the glorious counterpart to Babylon's judgment: a vial-angel invites John to see the bride, the Lamb's wife — the New Jerusalem, the glorified church, set over against the fallen harlot. The verse displays the bride, the descending holy city (v.10).
10:
"And he carried me away in the spirit to a great and high mountain, and shewed me that great city, the holy Jerusalem, descending out of heaven from God," The brief verse notes John shown the holy city from a high mountain. The grammatical voices note the vantage. Barnes and Robertson note the vision: "he carried me away in the spirit to a great and high mountain, and shewed me that great city, the holy Jerusalem, descending out of heaven from God" — John is carried in the Spirit to a great, high mountain (the vantage for viewing the city, as Ezekiel 40:2), and shown the holy Jerusalem descending from God; the glorified city/church is displayed in its descent.

On "a great and high mountain," the witnesses note the vantage-point. Barnes and Ellicott note that John is taken "to a great and high mountain" — the elevated vantage from which the whole city may be seen; the parallel to Ezekiel's vision of the city (Ezekiel 40:2); the place of revelation.

On "that great city, the holy Jerusalem, descending out of heaven from God," the grammatical-historical note the descending city: "that great city, the holy Jerusalem, descending out of heaven from God" — the holy city (v.2), of heavenly origin, comes down from God; the glorified church, the bride, displayed in her descent; the New Jerusalem is God's gift, from heaven, not of earthly making. The verse displays John shown the holy city from a high mountain.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the vision — "he carried me away in the spirit to a great and high mountain, and shewed me that great city, the holy Jerusalem, descending out of heaven from God," John shown the descending holy city from a high mountain (Ezekiel 40:2, Barnes, Robertson); "a great and high mountain" the vantage for viewing the city (Barnes, Ellicott); "the holy Jerusalem, descending out of heaven from God," the glorified city of heavenly origin (v.2, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the shown city. The verse marks the mountain: great and high. The verse displays the vantage: for the vision. The verse displays the city: holy Jerusalem. The verse displays the descent: from God. The vision displays the holy city shown descending from heaven. The verse displays the mountain: ascended. The verse displays the city: descending. The verse displays the origin: God. John shown the holy city from a high mountain displays the vantage of the bride-vision: John is carried to a great and high mountain and shown the holy Jerusalem descending out of heaven from God — the glorified city of heavenly origin, displayed in her descent. The verse displays the descending city, radiant with God's glory (v.11).
11:
"Having the glory of God: and her light was like unto a stone most precious, even like a jasper stone, clear as crystal;" The brief verse notes the city radiant with the glory of God. The grammatical voices note her splendor. Barnes and Robertson note the city's glory: "having the glory of God: and her light was like unto a stone most precious, even like a jasper stone, clear as crystal" — the holy city has the glory of God (the divine presence, his shekinah, illumining her), and her light (radiance) is like a most precious stone, a jasper, clear as crystal; the city shines with the very glory of God.

On "having the glory of God," the witnesses note the divine presence. Barnes and Ellicott note that the city "having the glory of God" — God's own glory is her light and splendor; the presence of God illumines the city (v.23); her radiance is not her own but God's glory shining in her.

On "her light was like unto a stone most precious... like a jasper stone, clear as crystal," the eastern Cyril and grammatical-historical note the brilliance: her light is "like a jasper stone, clear as crystal" — a brilliant, translucent, precious radiance; the figure of pure, dazzling, glorious light; the city is transparent with the glory of God, radiant as a flawless jewel. The verse displays the city radiant with the glory of God.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the glory — "having the glory of God: and her light was like unto a stone most precious, even like a jasper stone, clear as crystal," the city radiant with God's glory, shining like a flawless jasper (Barnes, Robertson); "having the glory of God" the divine presence illumining her (v.23, Barnes, Ellicott); "like a jasper stone, clear as crystal," the pure, dazzling, translucent radiance (Cyril). The verse displays the radiant city. The verse marks the glory: of God. The verse displays the light: a precious stone. The verse displays the jasper: clear as crystal. The verse displays the radiance: dazzling. The vision displays the city shining with God's own glory. The verse displays the glory: God's. The verse displays the light: jasper. The verse displays the crystal: clear. The city radiant with the glory of God displays the splendor of the New Jerusalem: she has the glory of God, and her light is like a most precious jasper stone, clear as crystal — the city shining with the very glory of God, radiant as a flawless jewel. The verse displays the radiant city, with her walls and gates (v.12).
12:
"And had a wall great and high, and had twelve gates, and at the gates twelve angels, and names written thereon, which are the names of the twelve tribes of the children of Israel:" The brief verse notes the city's wall and twelve gates of the tribes. The grammatical voices note the wall and gates. Barnes and Robertson note the description: "a wall great and high, and had twelve gates, and at the gates twelve angels, and names written thereon, which are the names of the twelve tribes" — the city has a great, high wall (security and separation) with twelve gates, twelve angels at the gates, and the names of the twelve tribes of Israel written on the gates; the city is secure, accessible by twelve gates, and bears the names of God's covenant people.

On "a wall great and high, and... twelve gates," the witnesses note the structure. Barnes and JFB note that the "wall great and high" marks the city's security and the exclusion of all evil (v.27), and the "twelve gates" mark its full accessibility (three on each side, v.13); the city is both safe and open.

On "twelve angels, and... the names of the twelve tribes," the grammatical-historical note the tribes: at the gates are "twelve angels" (guardians) and "the names of the twelve tribes of the children of Israel" — the gates bear the tribes' names (as Ezekiel 48:31); the city is connected with the covenant people of the Old Testament; the twelve tribes (with the twelve apostles, v.14) mark the unity of the redeemed of both covenants. The verse displays the city's wall and twelve gates of the tribes.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the structure — "a wall great and high, and had twelve gates, and at the gates twelve angels, and names... of the twelve tribes," the secure, twelve-gated city bearing the tribes' names (Barnes, Robertson); "a wall great and high... twelve gates" the city's security and accessibility (v.27, Barnes, JFB); "twelve angels, and... the names of the twelve tribes," the gates of the tribes, the covenant people (Ezekiel 48:31, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the gated city. The verse marks the wall: great and high. The verse displays the gates: twelve. The verse displays the angels: at the gates. The verse displays the names: the twelve tribes. The structure displays the city secure and bearing the tribes' names. The verse displays the wall: high. The verse displays the gates: twelve. The verse displays the tribes: named. The city's wall and twelve gates of the tribes display the structure of the New Jerusalem: a great, high wall with twelve gates, twelve angels at the gates, and the names of the twelve tribes of Israel inscribed — the secure city, accessible by twelve gates, bearing the names of the covenant people. The verse displays the gated city, with gates on every side (v.13).
13:
"On the east three gates; on the north three gates; on the south three gates; and on the west three gates." The very brief verse notes the twelve gates on the four sides. The grammatical voices note the symmetrical arrangement. Barnes and Robertson note the disposition: "On the east three gates; on the north three gates; on the south three gates; and on the west three gates" — the twelve gates are arranged three on each of the four sides; the symmetrical, complete arrangement (drawn from Ezekiel 48:31-34); the city is open on every side, accessible from all directions.

On "three gates" on each side, the witnesses note the completeness. Barnes and Gill note that three gates on each of the four sides mark the perfect, symmetrical order of the city and its full accessibility from every quarter; the redeemed come from all directions (Matthew 8:11; Luke 13:29) into the city.

On the four sides, the grammatical-historical note the universal access: the gates on east, north, south, and west mark the city's openness to all the redeemed from every part of the earth; the comprehensive, universal access to the holy city; none of the saved is shut out by distance or direction. The verse displays the twelve gates on the four sides.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. 14:
"And the wall of the city had twelve foundations, and in them the names of the twelve apostles of the Lamb." The brief verse notes the twelve foundations of the apostles. The grammatical voices note the foundations. Barnes and Robertson note the description: "the wall of the city had twelve foundations, and in them the names of the twelve apostles of the Lamb" — the city's wall has twelve foundations, bearing the names of the twelve apostles of the Lamb; the church is built on the foundation of the apostles (Ephesians 2:20), and their names are honored in the foundations of the holy city.

On "twelve foundations," the witnesses note the apostolic foundation. Barnes and Ellicott note that the "twelve foundations" mark the apostolic foundation of the church — built on the apostles' teaching and testimony (Ephesians 2:20; Matthew 16:18); the foundations bearing their names honor the apostles' place in the city of God.

On "the names of the twelve apostles of the Lamb," the grammatical-historical note the apostles. Barnes and Gill note that "the twelve apostles of the Lamb" are named in the foundations, as the twelve tribes are on the gates (v.12) — the redeemed of both covenants (Israel's tribes and Christ's apostles) are united in the one city; the New Jerusalem honors both the old covenant people (gates) and the apostolic church (foundations). The verse displays the twelve foundations of the apostles.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the foundations — "the wall of the city had twelve foundations, and in them the names of the twelve apostles of the Lamb," the apostolic foundations of the city (Ephesians 2:20, Barnes, Robertson); "twelve foundations" the apostolic foundation of the church (Matthew 16:18, Barnes, Ellicott); "the names of the twelve apostles," the apostles honored, with the tribes (v.12), uniting both covenants (Barnes, Gill). 15:
"And he that talked with me had a golden reed to measure the city, and the gates thereof, and the wall thereof." The very brief verse notes the angel with a golden reed to measure the city. The grammatical voices note the measuring. Barnes and Robertson note the instrument: "he that talked with me had a golden reed to measure the city, and the gates thereof, and the wall thereof" — the angel has a golden measuring-reed to measure the city, its gates, and its wall (as Ezekiel 40:3; Zechariah 2:1); the measuring marks the perfect proportions and the completeness of the city.

On "a golden reed to measure the city," the witnesses note the measuring's purpose. Barnes and Ellicott note that the "golden reed" (the measuring-rod, golden for its preciousness) is used to measure the city, gates, and wall — to display its exact, perfect dimensions; the measuring reveals the city's symmetry and completeness.

On the measuring, the grammatical-historical note the significance: the measuring of the city (vv.16-17) shows its perfect, complete, and ordered form; nothing is wanting or amiss; the golden reed reveals the divine perfection of the holy city's proportions. The verse displays the angel with a golden reed to measure the city.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. 16:
"And the city lieth foursquare, and the length is as large as the breadth: and he measured the city with the reed, twelve thousand furlongs. The length and the breadth and the height of it are equal." The witnesses note the city foursquare, vast, and equal in dimensions. On "the city lieth foursquare, and the length is as large as the breadth," the grammatical voices note the perfect square. Barnes and Robertson note the form: "the city lieth foursquare, and the length is as large as the breadth" — the city is a perfect square (foursquare), its length and breadth equal; the figure of perfect symmetry and order (as the Holy of Holies was a cube); the city's regular, perfect form.

On "he measured the city with the reed, twelve thousand furlongs," the witnesses note the vast size. Barnes and Ellicott note that the city measures "twelve thousand furlongs (stadia, about 1,400-1,500 miles)" — an immense, symbolic dimension; the vast size (twelve thousand, the number of the tribes multiplied) marks the greatness and capacity of the city; large enough for all the redeemed.

On "the length and the breadth and the height of it are equal," the grammatical-historical note the cube: "the length and the breadth and the height of it are equal" — the city is a perfect cube (length, breadth, height equal), like the Holy of Holies (1 Kings 6:20); the figure of perfect, complete holiness and symmetry; the whole city is a sanctuary, a perfect cube of glory. The verse displays the city foursquare, vast, and equal in dimensions.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the form — "the city lieth foursquare, and the length is as large as the breadth," the perfect square of symmetry (Barnes, Robertson); "twelve thousand furlongs," the immense, symbolic size, large for all the redeemed (Barnes, Ellicott); "the length and the breadth and the height of it are equal," the perfect cube, like the Holy of Holies (1 Kings 6:20, grammatical-historical). 17:
"And he measured the wall thereof, an hundred and forty and four cubits, according to the measure of a man, that is, of the angel." The brief verse notes the measure of the city's wall. The grammatical voices note the wall's measure. Barnes and Robertson note the measuring: "he measured the wall thereof, an hundred and forty and four cubits, according to the measure of a man, that is, of the angel" — the wall measures 144 cubits (twelve times twelve, the symbolic number of the tribes/apostles); the measuring is "according to the measure of a man, that is, of the angel" — the human standard of measure used by the angel.

On "an hundred and forty and four cubits," the witnesses note the symbolic number. Barnes and Ellicott note that "144 cubits" (12 × 12) is the symbolic number associated with the people of God (the twelve tribes and twelve apostles, the 144,000); the wall's measure echoes the number of the redeemed; whether the wall's height or thickness, the number is significant.

On "according to the measure of a man, that is, of the angel," the grammatical-historical note the standard: the measuring is "according to the measure of a man, that is, of the angel" — the angel measures by the ordinary human standard (the cubit); the measure is intelligible to men; the angel uses the human unit of measurement. The verse displays the measure of the city's wall.

The very brief comment matches the verse. 18:
"And the building of the wall of it was of jasper: and the city was pure gold, like unto clear glass." The brief verse notes the wall of jasper and the city of pure gold. Barnes and Robertson note the description: "the building of the wall of it was of jasper: and the city was pure gold, like unto clear glass" — the wall is built of jasper (the precious, brilliant stone, v.11), and the city itself is pure gold, transparent like clear glass; the figure of the city's surpassing preciousness, purity, and brilliance.

On "the wall of it was of jasper," the witnesses note the wall's material. Barnes and Ellicott note that the "wall of jasper" — the jasper being the stone of the city's glory (v.11) — marks the wall's brilliant, precious beauty; the city is enclosed in a wall of dazzling jewel.

On "the city was pure gold, like unto clear glass," the Reformed Gill and grammatical-historical note the transparent gold: "the city was pure gold, like unto clear glass" — the city is pure gold, yet transparent as clear glass; the figure of perfect purity and brilliance, gold so refined it is translucent; the surpassing, glorious purity of the holy city, beyond any earthly gold. The verse displays the wall of jasper and the city of pure gold.

19:

On "garnished with all manner of precious stones," the witnesses note the splendor. Barnes and Ellicott note that the foundations "garnished with all manner of precious stones" — adorned with every kind of jewel (recalling the high priest's breastplate, Exodus 28:17-20); the apostolic foundations gleam with precious stones; the figure of glory and worth.

On "jasper... sapphire... chalcedony... emerald," the grammatical-historical note the specific gems: the named stones (jasper, sapphire, chalcedony, emerald, and the rest, v.20) mark each foundation with a distinct, precious jewel; the variety and brilliance of the gems display the manifold glory of the city's foundations; each apostolic foundation is a precious stone. The verse displays the jeweled foundations of the city.

20:

The brief verse notes the remaining jeweled foundations. The grammatical voices note the continued list. 21:

The brief verse notes the pearl gates and the golden street. The grammatical voices note the gates and street. Barnes and Robertson note the description: "the twelve gates were twelve pearls; every several gate was of one pearl: and the street of the city was pure gold, as it were transparent glass" — each of the twelve gates is a single pearl, and the city's street is pure gold, transparent as glass; the figure of the surpassing, costly beauty of the city — gates of pearl, streets of transparent gold.

On "the twelve gates were twelve pearls; every several gate was of one pearl," the witnesses note the pearl gates. Barnes and Ellicott note that each gate is "of one pearl" — a single, immense pearl (the pearl being a costly, beautiful gem, Matthew 13:46); the gates of the city are of surpassing preciousness; the entrance to the holy city is through gates of pearl.

On "the street of the city was pure gold, as it were transparent glass," the grammatical-historical note the golden street: "the street of the city was pure gold, as it were transparent glass" — the city's street (or broad way) is pure, transparent gold (v.18); the figure of the perfect purity and brilliance underfoot; the very streets are of translucent gold; the surpassing glory of the city in every part. 22:
"And I saw no temple therein: for the Lord God Almighty and the Lamb are the temple of it." The witnesses note that God and the Lamb are the temple of the city. On "I saw no temple therein," the grammatical voices note the absence of a temple. Barnes and Robertson note the striking feature: "I saw no temple therein: for the Lord God Almighty and the Lamb are the temple of it" — there is no temple in the holy city; in pointed contrast to the earthly Jerusalem (whose glory was its temple), the New Jerusalem has none; for God and the Lamb are themselves its temple.

On "no temple therein," the witnesses note the meaning. Barnes and Henry note that the absence of a temple marks the perfect, immediate presence of God; in the old order, the temple was the place of God's special presence and the means of approach; in the new, God is immediately present everywhere; no special structure is needed, for all is holy.

On "the Lord God Almighty and the Lamb are the temple of it," the puritan Henry and grammatical-historical note the divine temple: "the Lord God Almighty and the Lamb are the temple of it" — God and the Lamb are themselves the temple; the redeemed worship and commune with God directly, without mediating structure; the immediate, unhindered presence of God and Christ is the city's sanctuary; the consummation of all that the temple foreshadowed. The verse displays that God and the Lamb are the temple of the city.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the absence — "I saw no temple therein: for the Lord God Almighty and the Lamb are the temple of it," no temple, for God and the Lamb are its temple (Barnes, Robertson); "no temple therein" the perfect, immediate presence of God, no mediating structure needed (Barnes, Henry); "the Lord God Almighty and the Lamb are the temple of it," God and Christ themselves the sanctuary (Henry). The verse displays the templeless city, needing no sun (v.23).
23:
"And the city had no need of the sun, neither of the moon, to shine in it: for the glory of God did lighten it, and the Lamb is the light thereof." The witnesses note that the glory of God and the Lamb are the city's light. On "the city had no need of the sun, neither of the moon, to shine in it," the grammatical voices note the absence of created lights. Barnes and Robertson note the feature: "the city had no need of the sun, neither of the moon, to shine in it" — the holy city needs no sun or moon; the created lights are superseded; in the new order, no natural luminaries are required; the city has a greater light.

On "the glory of God did lighten it," the witnesses note the divine light. Barnes and Ellicott note that "the glory of God did lighten it" — God's own glory (his shekinah, v.11) illumines the city (Isaiah 60:19-20); the divine glory is the city's light; God's presence is its sun.

On "the Lamb is the light thereof," the grammatical-historical note Christ the light: "the Lamb is the light (lamp) thereof" — the Lamb (Christ) is the lamp of the city; God's glory shines, and the Lamb is the light-bearer through whom it shines; Christ, the light of the world (John 8:12), is the everlasting light of the New Jerusalem; the redeemed walk in the light of God and the Lamb. The verse displays that the glory of God and the Lamb are the city's light.

The thin comment matches the verse. 24:
"And the nations of them which are saved shall walk in the light of it: and the kings of the earth do bring their glory and honour into it." The witnesses note the nations and kings bringing their glory into the city. On "the nations of them which are saved shall walk in the light of it," the grammatical voices note the saved nations. Barnes and Robertson note the vision: "the nations of them which are saved shall walk in the light of it" — the saved nations walk in the light of the city (Isaiah 60:3); the redeemed of all nations live and walk in the light of God and the Lamb; the universal scope of the city's blessing, embracing the saved from all peoples.

Barnes and Ellicott note that "the nations of them which are saved" (or simply "the nations") — the redeemed from all nations — walk in the city's light; the holy city is the home of the saved from every people; the universal gathering of the redeemed.

On "the kings of the earth do bring their glory and honour into it," the eastern Cyril and grammatical-historical note the homage: "the kings of the earth do bring their glory and honour into it" — the kings of the earth bring their glory and honor into the city (Isaiah 60:3-11); the figure of all earthly glory laid at the feet of God in the holy city; the redeemed kings (in contrast to the kings who served Babylon) bring their honor to the New Jerusalem; all glory is rendered to God. The verse displays the nations and kings bringing their glory into the city.

The thin comment matches the verse. 25:
"And the gates of it shall not be shut at all by day: for there shall be no night there." The very brief verse notes the gates never shut and the absence of night. The grammatical voices note the open gates. Barnes and Robertson note the feature: "the gates of it shall not be shut at all by day: for there shall be no night there" — the city's gates are never shut, for there is no night; the gates, never closed, mark the perfect security and the constant, free access to the city; in a world without enemies, no gate need ever be shut.

On "the gates of it shall not be shut at all by day," the witnesses note the perpetual openness. Barnes and Ellicott note that the gates "shall not be shut at all" — unlike earthly cities, which shut their gates at night against danger, the holy city's gates stand always open; perfect safety and welcome; free, perpetual access to the city of God.

On "for there shall be no night there," the grammatical-historical note the endless day: "for there shall be no night there" — there is no night in the holy city (v.23; 22:5); the perpetual day of God's light; no darkness, no time of closing; the figure of unending light, security, and joy; the gates need never close because the day never ends. The verse displays the gates never shut and the absence of night.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the open gates — "the gates of it shall not be shut at all by day: for there shall be no night there," the gates never shut, for there is no night, marking perfect security and access (Barnes, Robertson); "the gates... shall not be shut at all" the perpetual openness, perfect safety (Barnes, Ellicott); "for there shall be no night there," the endless day of God's light (v.23; 22:5, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the open city. The verse marks the gates: never shut. The verse displays the day: perpetual. The verse displays the night: none. The verse displays the security: perfect. The feature displays the city ever open in endless day. The verse displays the gates: open. The verse displays the night: gone. The verse displays the day: unending. The gates never shut and the absence of night display the security and light of the New Jerusalem: the gates are never shut, for there is no night there — perfect safety, free access, and the unending day of God's light. The verse displays the open city, into which the glory of nations is brought (v.26).
26:
"And they shall bring the glory and honour of the nations into it." The very brief verse notes the glory of the nations brought into the city. The grammatical voices note the homage. Barnes and Robertson note the gathering of glory: "they shall bring the glory and honour of the nations into it" — the glory and honor of the nations are brought into the holy city (resuming v.24; Isaiah 60:5, 11); all that is excellent and honorable among the nations is gathered into the city of God; the redeemed nations render their glory to God.

On "the glory and honour of the nations," the witnesses note the offered excellence. Barnes and Ellicott note that "the glory and honour of the nations" — the best, the noblest, the most excellent of the nations — is brought in; whatever is truly glorious and honorable is consecrated to God in the holy city; the nations' glory finds its home and end in the New Jerusalem.

On the bringing in, the grammatical-historical note the consecration: the nations' glory is "brought into it" — laid up in the city of God; all true glory is rendered to God and gathered into his city; the redeemed peoples consecrate their honor to God; the universal homage of the nations to the holy city. The verse displays the glory of the nations brought into the city.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the homage — "they shall bring the glory and honour of the nations into it," the nations' glory gathered into the city (v.24; Isaiah 60:5, 11, Barnes, Robertson); "the glory and honour of the nations" the best and noblest brought in (Barnes, Ellicott); the glory consecrated to God in his city (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the gathered glory. The verse marks the glory: of the nations. The verse displays the honor: brought in. The verse displays the consecration: to God. The verse displays the homage: universal. The bringing displays the nations' glory rendered to God in the city. The verse displays the glory: gathered. The verse displays the nations: honoring. The verse displays the city: enriched. The glory of the nations brought into the city displays the universal homage to the New Jerusalem: the glory and honor of the nations are brought into it — all that is excellent among the nations consecrated to God and gathered into his holy city. The verse displays the honored city, into which nothing unclean enters (v.27).
27:
"And there shall in no wise enter into it any thing that defileth, neither whatsoever worketh abomination, or maketh a lie: but they which are written in the Lamb's book of life." The witnesses note that nothing unclean enters, only those in the Lamb's book of life. On "there shall in no wise enter into it any thing that defileth," the grammatical voices note the exclusion of evil. Barnes and Robertson note the purity: "there shall in no wise enter into it any thing that defileth, neither whatsoever worketh abomination, or maketh a lie" — nothing unclean, no one who works abomination or falsehood, can enter the holy city; the perfect purity of the New Jerusalem; all defilement, abomination, and lying are wholly excluded.

On "any thing that defileth... abomination, or... a lie," the witnesses note the excluded. Barnes and Ellicott note that "any thing that defileth, neither whatsoever worketh abomination, or maketh a lie" — every defiling, abominable, and false thing is shut out; the city admits nothing unholy; the contrast with Babylon (full of abomination, 17:4-5); the holy city is wholly clean.

On "but they which are written in the Lamb's book of life," the grammatical-historical note the admitted: only "they which are written in the Lamb's book of life" enter — the redeemed, whose names are in the book of life (20:12, 15; 13:8); the book of life is the test of entrance; only the saved, the citizens of the city, may enter; the redeemed alone inhabit the holy city. The verse displays that nothing unclean enters, only those in the Lamb's book of life.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the purity — "there shall in no wise enter into it any thing that defileth, neither whatsoever worketh abomination, or maketh a lie," nothing unclean or false enters the holy city (Barnes, Robertson); "any thing that defileth... abomination... a lie" all impurity excluded, in contrast to Babylon (17:4-5, Barnes, Ellicott); "but they which are written in the Lamb's book of life," only the redeemed, in the book of life, enter (20:12, 15, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the pure city. The verse marks the unclean: excluded. The verse displays the abomination: shut out. The verse displays the admitted: the book of life. The verse displays the citizens: the redeemed. The exclusion displays the holy city pure, entered only by the saved. The verse displays the defilement: barred. The verse displays the book: of life. The verse displays the redeemed: admitted. That nothing unclean enters, only those in the Lamb's book of life, displays the perfect purity of the New Jerusalem: nothing that defiles, no abomination or lie, can enter — only those written in the Lamb's book of life; the holy city wholly clean, inhabited by the redeemed alone. The verse displays the pure city, with the river and tree of life (ch.22).
